channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oscars 2026 Predictions: Who Will Win Best Picture, Actor and Actress?
item.description: “Sinners” and “One Battle After Another” have been neck and neck all season. Here’s what our expert thinks will go the distance.
item.dc:creator: Kyle Buchanan

item.title: The Secret History of Rock’s Wildest Stage Prop: P-Funk’s Mothership
item.description: A half-century ago, George Clinton debuted a $500,000 spaceship that turned his live concerts into intergalactic journeys. Here’s how it went down.
item.dc:creator: Steve Knopper
item.media:description: Clinton said he told his label boss, “Get the Mothership,” and that he replied, “Oh, hell yeah.”

item.title: Bill Cosby, Out of Prison and the Public Eye, Faces Civil Trial
item.description: The entertainer whose conviction was overturned in 2021 still faces sex assault lawsuits from multiple women, including one playing out now in a California court.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley
item.media:description: In a civil trial underway in California, a woman is accusing Bill Cosby of drugging and sexually assaulting her in 1972. He has denied the allegations.

item.title: Chilean Architect Smiljan Radic Wins Pritzker Prize
item.description: The naming of Smiljan Radic as the next Pritzker laureate was delayed by the disclosure of Jeffrey Epstein’s friendship with Tom Pritzker, who has now distanced himself from the prize.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin

item.title: Live Nation Slacks Reveal Employees Joking About ‘Stupid’ Fans
item.description: A series of private exchanges in the messaging system Slack were revealed as exhibits in the Justice Department’s antitrust lawsuit against the concert giant.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: Live Nation had argued that the exchanges should be excluded from the evidence in the trial, saying they were “irrelevant” private remarks.

item.title: Maya Rudolph to Make Broadway Debut in “Oh, Mary!”
item.description: The actress will make her Broadway debut in a role that, she said, “feels like a badge of honor.”
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:description: Maya Rudolph is stepping into “Oh, Mary!” on April 28. There’s a freedom to the role, she said: “Mary can just be her worst, and all over the place, and at the same time incredibly fun to watch.”

item.title: On the Hunt for 24 Hours of Drag in N.Y.C.
item.description: An enthusiast went on a madcap adventure to find some fun events for all types across the city, from bingo fans to “Star Trek” aficionados.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg and Amy Lombard
item.media:credit: Amy Lombard for The New York Times

item.title: In Shaker Design, a Zeal for No Zeal
item.description: Straight chairs, whirling dances: The austere craftsmanship of this disappearing group is as striking as their ecstatic worship, on view at ICA Philadelphia.
item.dc:creator: Christopher Hawthorne
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left: a swivel chair from the Shaker community in Mount Lebanon, N.Y., circa 1860; an elder’s rocking chair, circa 1850-1870; a child’s armed rocking chair with tape seat, circa 1880; a side table, circa 1840.

item.title: Histories of Native American Treaties and Anti-Chinese Violence Win Bancroft Prize
item.description: The award, one of the most prestigious in the field of American history, honors “scope, significance, depth of research and richness of interpretation.”
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler
item.category: Vested Interests: Trusteeship and Native Dispossession in the United States (Book)
John Doe Chinaman: A Forgotten History of Chinese Life Under American Racial Law (Book)

item.title: ‘Bushido’ Review: A Samurai’s Dangerous Moves
item.description: In this period drama, a vagrant samurai slays with a sword and his mastery of the game Go.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: Bughouse’ Review: Inside Henry Darger’s Unwieldy Mind
item.description: Henry Darger, a recluse who left behind thousands of wild illustrations upon his death, is the subject of fascination in this one-man show.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: ‘Undertone’ Review: And Now a Word From Our Demon!
item.description: An effective if occasionally bumpy debut horror film makes full use of the audience’s ears — and imagination.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: The Titan of Land Art Moves Indoors and Gets Intimate
item.description: Michael Heizer, renowned for monumental earthworks like “City,” offers a domesticated, rattlesnake-proof art for Manhattan’s Gagosian.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: Top-End Auction Sales Help Pull Global Art Market Out of Slump, Study Says
item.description: The top drivers included a $2.2 billion auction week in New York and strong fall fairs, according to the annual Art Basel and UBS report.
item.dc:creator: Scott Reyburn
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Art Basel Paris fair at the Grand Palais last October.

item.title: Late Night Unwraps ‘War’ vs. ‘Excursion’ vs. ‘Both’
item.description: The “Late Night” host Seth Meyers said President Trump is letting Americans “choose your own adventure” in the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Daniel Radcliffe Shines in ‘Every Brilliant Thing’ on Broadway
item.description: The actor’s fondness for the audience radiates outward in this delightful interactive play about naming and noticing the good in the world.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:description: Daniel Radcliffe, center right, with audience members in “Every Brilliant Thing” at the Hudson Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: ‘The Pitt’ Season 2, Episode 10 Recap: Invisible String
item.description: Family ties bind the characters together in this week’s episode, in ways both beautiful and suffocating.
item.dc:creator: Sean T. Collins
item.media:description: Dr. Mohan (Supriya Ganesh) had a panic attack, brought on partly by the incessant calling of her mother.

item.title: Andrea Constand Testifies in Support of Another Bill Cosby Accuser
item.description: Lawyers for a woman who has sued Mr. Cosby, accusing him of sexual assault, called Ms. Constand as a witness in the case, which is now being tried in California.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Graham Bowley
item.media:description: Andrea Constand, whose testimony led to Bill Cosby’s conviction that was later overturned, testified in a civil case brought by another Cosby accuser.

item.title: ‘Antigone (This Play I Read in High School)’ Review: Tragically Uneven
item.description: Anna Ziegler’s feminist take on Sophocles tries to tie in reproductive politics, but the play keeps trampling over its own ideas.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Antigone (This Play I Read in High School) (Play)
item.media:description: Susannah Perkins, left, and Celia Keenan-Bolger in “Antigone (This Play I Read in High School)” at the Public Theater in Manhattan.

item.title: Roscoe Robinson, Gospel Star Who Minted 1960s R&B Hits, Dies at 97
item.description: Although he wasn’t blind, he sang in three different gospel groups known as the Blind Boys before making a splash on the R&B and pop charts.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: Roscoe Robinson in an undated photo. The singer Sam Cooke was an early admirer and helped jump-start his career, telling one gospel group, “’Scoe is the guy you need.”

item.title: Pussycat Dolls Announce New Single and Reunion Tour
item.description: You’re in luck: The early 2000s girl group announced a new single and a reunion tour on Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Claire Moses
item.media:credit: Vince Bucci/Getty Images for Maui and Sons
item.media:description: Nicole Scherzinger, center, and the Pussycat Dolls performing in 2008.

item.title: Remembering Marilyn Monroe, Draped In Diamonds and Coveting More
item.description: Events marking the birth centennial of the person who became Marilyn Monroe will feature jewelry — though she was rarely associated with expensive pieces offscreen.
item.dc:creator: David Belcher
item.media:description: Marilyn Monroe, wearing lots of diamonds, in a publicity photo for “Gentlemen Prefer Blondes” in which she sang “Diamonds Are a Girl’s Best Friend” wearing the same outfit and jewelry.

item.title: 5 Classical Music Albums You Can Listen to Right Now
item.description: Lise Davidsen’s recital at the Met Opera, Gustavo Dudamel leading “Romeo and Juliet,” and a violinist’s personal program are among our selections.

item.title: A New Oscar for Best Casting
item.description: This year, there’s a new category at the Oscars: the Academy Award for best achievement in casting. Our reporter Esther Zuckerman spoke with all five nominees to find out what makes good casting.
item.dc:creator: Esther Zuckerman, Léo Hamelin, Laura Salaberry and Edward Vega

item.title: A Giant Pigeon Is Leaving the High Line
item.description: “Dinosaur,” a sculpture, has been in residence for 18 months. It has its fans who are sad to see it depart.
item.dc:creator: James Barron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: ‘Space Cowboy’ Review: A Sky-diving Cinematographer’s Highs and Lows
item.description: This documentary about the ace sky-diving cameraman Joe Jennings unexpectedly, but meaningfully, looks at the man’s struggle with depression.
item.dc:creator: Nicolas Rapold

item.title: ‘Slanted’ Review: Narratively Botched
item.description: This body horror thriller, about a Chinese American girl who undergoes a surgery to appear white, relies on dated tropes to construct a pale genre pastiche.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: ‘Reminders of Him’ Review: A Second Chance at Love
item.description: The movie, based on a Colleen Hoover book, is an unabashed tear-jerker. Terrific lead actors Maika Monroe and Tyriq Withers make it work.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘Group: The Schopenhauer Effect’ Review: The Art of Being Patient
item.description: Group therapy sessions are dramatized in this absorbing film from the writer-director Alexis Lloyd.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: Dark and Stormy Thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and More
item.description: The best-selling author Lisa Unger recommends her favorite dark and stormy thrillers by Stephen King, Ruth Ware and more.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Unger

item.title: Jimmy Kimmel Addresses FBI Warnings for Potential Iranian Drone Strike Ahead of Oscars
item.description: “Isn’t this how ‘Ironman 3’ started?” Kimmel said after the F.B.I. warned state officials to prepare for a retaliatory Iranian drone strike on the West Coast ahead of Sunday’s Academy Awards.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)

item.title: Haitian President’s Widow Recalls Killers Spoke Spanish and Used Nicknames
item.description: The prosecution used testimony by Martine Moïse about the language she heard on the night her husband was killed to support its claim that the assassination was carried out by a hired Colombian hit squad.
item.dc:creator: David C. Adams
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Martine Moïse, left, and her son in Port-au-Prince, Haiti, in 2021, after the attack that killed her husband.

item.title: Stephen Koch, 84, Dies; Champion of a Belatedly Hailed Photographer
item.description: A writer and critic, Mr. Koch struggled for years to shepherd his friend Peter Hujar’s underappreciated, Bohemian-world artwork to posthumous glory.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Peter Schneider Dies at 85; His Novels Explored a Divided Germany
item.description: His best-known work, “The Wall Jumper,” proved prescient in its contention that the country would remain split even after reunification.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:description: Peter Schneider in 1994. He was considered one of the most important literary figures of his generation, in part because his life was so intertwined with the postwar German experience.

item.title: Angelika Saleh, the Angelika of Angelika Film Center, Dies at 90
item.description: After making the journey from prewar Germany to Madison Avenue opulence, she gave her name to one of New York’s most influential indie cinemas.
item.dc:creator: Alex Traub

item.title: Quentin Tarantino Plans ‘Swashbuckling’ Play for London’s West End
item.description: “The Popinjay Cavalier,” written and directed by Tarantino, will open next year. It is described as a “rambunctious comedy of deception” set in 1830s Europe.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Quentin Tarantino’s foray into theater comes several years after the release of his ninth feature film, “Once Upon a Time … in Hollywood,” in 2019. He has long hinted that he will retire after making 10 films.

item.title: In Japan, Making Wooden Kokeshi Dolls
item.description: A Japanese artisan demonstrates the skill needed to produce kokeshi.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Morelli and Andrew Faulk
item.media:description: The family of Teruyuki Hiraga has been making kokeshi, or traditional Japanese wooden dolls, for eight generations.

item.title: Minecraft’s Uncensored Library Adds a United States Wing
item.description: The Uncensored Library, a digital project that chronicles attacks on journalistic liberty, is adding a U.S. wing alongside those dedicated to Russia, Saudi Arabia and others.
item.dc:creator: Will Bahr

item.title: Dan Simmons, Genre-Leaping Author of ‘The Terror,’ Dies at 77
item.description: He moved easily and prolifically through science fiction, fantasy, horror, thrillers, crime and historical fiction. His book “The Terror” was made into a cable TV series.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir
item.media:description: Dan Simmons in 1996. “He said from the beginning that he wanted to write what he wanted to write,” his wife, Karen Simmons, said, “and if the publisher didn’t want him to go in that direction, he’d switch publishers.”

item.title: Through Violent Memes on Social Media, Trump Promotes the War on Iran
item.description: A series of White House social-media videos turn the carnage in Iran into gleeful entertainment.
item.dc:creator: James Poniewozik
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: A video released by the White House
item.media:description: A social media video released by the White House mixed pop culture clips with bombing footage.

item.title: To Pay the Bills, a Cult Band Becomes a Doors Tribute Act
item.description: With even critically acclaimed bands struggling to make a living, the British group Field Music has come up with a novel way to top up its income.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Joanna Yee
item.media:credit: Joanna Yee for The New York Times

item.title: Jessie Buckley Became a Mother for ‘Hamnet.’ Then She Became a Real One. (Encore)
item.description: The Oscar-nominated actress talks about the primal love and grief of motherhood.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Reva Goldberg, Emily Lang, Davis Land, Christina Djossa, Amy Pearl, Sara Curtis, Elisa Gutierrez, Jen Poyant, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez and Dan Powell
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by The New York Times; Background: Brian Rea; Inset photo: Chris Pizzello/Invision, via AP

item.title: At This Festival, the Dead of Winter Is the Life of the Party
item.description: In the Hudson Valley town of Chatham, N.Y., a weeklong event called “The Dark” took over theaters, bars, a church, a bookshop, a library and even a sauna.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Gilbertson
item.media:description: In the work by Schneider, above, an unseen narrator guides the audience through a matrix of 4,000 LED lights.

item.title: How ‘The Sopranos’ Came to Life
item.description: A new exhibit at the Museum of the Moving Image in Queens traces how the creators developed the look and themes of the show.
item.dc:creator: Sara Aridi

item.title: An Artist Renounced His Family. They Sued to Acquire His Life’s Work.
item.description: A settlement is reached in the case of Mike Disfarmer, who renounced his family. Decades later they sued to take back his life’s work. When heirs battle the people who built their legacies, the art may be at stake.
item.dc:creator: Arthur Lubow

item.title: Late Night Makes Fun of Pete Hegseth’s Fruit Baskets
item.description: The Pentagon reportedly spent lavishly on doughnuts, ice cream machines and stickers — “and despite all that, nobody came to Pete Hegseth’s birthday party,” Stephen Colbert said.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Suspect in Rihanna Shooting Is Charged With Attempted Murder
item.description: A woman fired a semiautomatic weapon at Rihanna’s home while she was there with ASAP Rocky and their three children on Sunday, the authorities said.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens
item.media:description: Nobody was injured in the shooting at Rihanna’s Los Angeles mansion on Sunday.

item.title: How ‘Sync Music’ Became the Soundtrack to Our Lives
item.description: “Sync music” has become the soundtrack to our lives — whether we realize it or not.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Francis Bradley
item.media:credit: Emily Shur for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kara Talve (left) and Anze Rozman, who are married to each other, collaborating on a composition at Bleeding Fingers studios in Santa Monica, Calif.

item.title: Bill Kurtis to Leave NPR’s ‘Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!’
item.description: As the irreverent quiz show’s judge and scorekeeper, Kurtis, a former news anchor known for his rich voice, has helped find comedy in current events since 2014.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:description: Bill Kurtis’s introductions on “Wait Wait … Don’t Tell Me!” have included “I’m the voice so creamy, you better take some Lactaid.”

item.title: Harry Styles and 8 More Boy Band Stars Who Went Solo
item.description: As the former One Direction singer releases his fourth album, listen back to how Bobby Brown, Ricky Martin and others made their own statements.
item.media:description: Harry Styles onstage at the Brit Awards earlier this month.

item.title: Dozens of States Weigh Continuing Live Nation Antitrust Case
item.description: After the Justice Department reached a settlement with the concert giant, the judge overseeing the trial asked the states and the company to do the same.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:description: The Justice Department, joined by 39 states and the District of Columbia, sued Live Nation in May 2024, arguing that the company was a monopoly that dominated the music industry.

item.title: Review: A New ‘Tristan und Isolde’ at the Metropolitan Opera
item.description: Yuval Sharon’s debut production featured an astonishing performance by the soprano Lise Davidsen. It is the event of the season.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:description: Michael Spyres, left, and Lise Davidsen in the tunnel as the title lovers in “Tristan und Isolde” at the Metropolitan Opera.

item.title: ‘Scarpetta’ Review: Nicole Kidman Stars in Long-Awaited Forensics Drama
item.description: Nicole Kidman stars in Prime Video’s adaptation of Patricia Cornwell’s long-running series of Kay Scarpetta novels.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: “Scarpetta” stars Nicole Kidman (with Bobby Cannavale) as the title character, a chief medical examiner.

item.title: New York Philharmonic Announces 2026-27 Season
item.description: A new era for the orchestra begins in September, when Gustavo Dudamel officially becomes its music and artistic director.
item.media:description: Gustavo Dudamel, who will officially become the New York Philharmonic’s music and artistic director in the 2026-27 season.

item.title: Alma Allen Signs With Mega-Gallery After Venice Biennale Fallout
item.description: Alma Allen has joined Perrotin, months after two other galleries dropped him over his selection to represent the United States at the “art world Olympics.”
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small
item.media:credit: Jake Naughton for The New York Times
item.media:description: Alma Allen at his home in Mexico City. The Utah-born sculptor, who lives and works in Mexico, will represent the United States at the 2026 Venice Biennale.

item.title: At 100, György Kurtág Is Still Searching for the Right Note
item.description: György Kurtág, the eminent composer and Hungarian national treasure, has reached a rare milestone yet shows little interest in retirement.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Akos Stiller for The New York Times
item.media:description: The composer György Kurtág in 2018. He recently celebrated his 100th birthday in Budapest.

item.title: ‘Fukushima: A Nuclear Nightmare’ Review: An Emotional Choice
item.description: This documentary offers a rundown of the 2011 nuclear meltdown with special attention to the shift workers who risked their lives while trying to stabilize the plant.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: Michelle Pfeiffer Makes It Look Easy. It Isn’t.
item.description: At 67, the actress is still reinventing herself, with two new TV shows, “The Madison” and “Margo’s Got Money Troubles,” and an evolving attitude about her work.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Soloski and Amy Harrity
item.media:credit: Amy Harrity for The New York Times

item.title: How the Oscars Best Picture Winner Is Chosen
item.description: Best picture is the only Oscar category decided by preferential ballot. Our reporter Marc Tracy explains how that works.
item.dc:creator: Marc Tracy, Léo Hamelin, Laura Salaberry and Lauren Pruitt

item.title: Book Review: ‘Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! A Memoir,’ by Liza Minnelli
item.description: “Kids, Wait Till You Hear This!” is a familiar reminder that growing up in showbiz can lead to awards and adulation, but also to heartache.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Kids, Wait Till You Hear This! (Book)
item.media:description: Liza Minnelli in 1972, the year she starred as Sally Bowles in “Cabaret.”

item.title: Late Night Scolds Trump Over Gasoline Prices
item.description: “You can lie about many things in American life, but one thing you can’t lie about is gas prices,” Late Night host Seth Meyers said. “Everyone sees it.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Show with Stephen Colbert (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
Real Time With Bill Maher (TV Program)

item.title: Fed Up With High Costs, American Theater Takes a Trip to London
item.description: Skyrocketing budgets and falling profitability have driven a new form of offshoring, with U.S. producers staging shows across the Atlantic.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.media:credit: Joshua Atkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: David Hunter as F. Scott Fitzgerald in a rehearsal for the new American musical “Beautiful Little Fool,” which had an initial run this winter at the Southwark Playhouse in London.
